text: We describe in this video our tips and tricks for doing an ambulatory supracervical hysterectomy in case of huge polimiomatous uterus in a virgin patient.

The surgeon begins the procedure by detaching the lateral attachment of a large uterus with multiple myomas. The left round ligament is cut using a harmonic scalpel. The mesosalpines are then cut, and a salpingectomy is performed, sparing both ovaries. The fallopian tube is cut close to the tube, away from the ovarian pedicle, to preserve ovarian vascularization.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to achieve hemostatic control before progressively cutting the adnexal pedicle, which facilitates the lateral release of the left ovary.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by addressing the lateral aspect of the uterus to ensure a clear view of both uterine pedicles. The same procedure is performed on the opposite side to maintain symmetry and visibility.

The surgeon cuts the right round ligament and the fallopian tube, while controlling the utero-ovarian pedicle close to the uterus. Coagulation is applied to the tissue at a level of two centimeters, followed by precise cutting in the middle to ensure a dry dissection and minimize ureteral injury.
The surgeon increases uterine mobility and proceeds to cut the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, moving toward the upper part of the uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon carefully avoids damaging the clearly visible uterine vein while cutting the peritoneum. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is dissected, revealing the large uterine vein below the harmonic scalpel. The vesico-vaginal peritoneum is tracked and cut in a dry procedure, enhancing uterine mobility and providing a clear view of the vaginal area.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine pedicle, exposing the vessel to apply bipolar energy directly, avoiding the surrounding peritoneum. Coagulation is applied over a three-centimeter area at the isthmic region, without cutting the pedicle to prevent backflow on the opposite side.

The surgeon controls the large right uterine artery and vein using bipolar energy, pulling the uterus to the left. The uterus is then cut at the isthmic level using a harmonic device, performing a supracervical hysterectomy without a uterine manipulator due to the patient's virgin status.

The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle using bipolar energy and cuts the pedicle with cold scissors, ensuring a bloodless procedure. The supracervical hysterectomy is completed without hemorrhage. The right fallopian tube is removed to reduce the risk of residual ovarian cancer.
The surgeon visually identifies and controls the right ureter, which is clearly visible, demonstrating ureteral rotation during the procedure.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy using a Vipril-O suture, employing an intracorporeal knotting technique to secure the ovary and reduce the risk of ovarian torsion, especially in the presence of a large uterus.
The surgeon performs an ovariopexy on the opposite side, using a large needle to facilitate the procedure and lock the knot with a specific technique.

The surgeon prepares for morcellation by inserting a 15mm morcellator into the left iliac fossa. The setup is adjusted to 700 rotations per minute for the procedure, which involves the gradual reduction of the uterine tissue mass.
This is the final aspect of our surgery. I hope this video was useful for my young colleague. Thank you for your great attention.
